title: Deconstructing the Deconstructor: When the Method Eats Itself

source.original: Today I Deconstruct Deconstruction — Brivael Le Pogam

body_html:
There is a deliberate irony at the heart of Le Pogam's project: he claims to "deconstruct deconstruction." This rhetorical jiu-jitsu — turning a method against itself — is not new, but its deployment in a viral social media thread gives it fresh cultural significance. The gesture acknowledges the power of the tool even as it seeks to dismantle it.

This self-referential move has a long pedigree. Critics of deconstruction have often argued that the method, if applied consistently, must eventually undermine its own foundations. If all texts contain internal contradictions and no meaning is stable, then deconstruction's own claims are equally unstable. Derrida himself was aware of this recursion and embraced it as part of the method's logic — deconstruction was never meant to stand outside the systems it analyzed.

What is new is the populist appropriation of this maneuver. Le Pogam is not writing from within the academy; he is performing a cultural operation on social media, compressing a sophisticated philosophical problem into a provocative thread designed for maximum engagement. The act of "deconstructing deconstruction" becomes itself a memetic weapon — a signal that the right has learned to speak the enemy's language.

This development sits within a broader trend visible in figures like Jordan Peterson, James Lindsay, and Helen Pluckrose, who have built significant audiences by framing postmodern philosophy as the root cause of contemporary cultural dysfunction. The shared strategy is to use accessible, combative rhetoric to delegitimize entire intellectual traditions. Whether this constitutes genuine critique or strategic oversimplification depends on where one stands — but its cultural impact is undeniable.
